coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots directly generated from graphite via magnetron sputtering and the application in thin-film transistors
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Huihui
Liu, Ao
Xu, Yuanhong
Shan, Fukai
Li, Aihua
Wang, Jianmei
Yang, Wenrong
Barrow, Colin
Liu, Jingquan
coredata.dc:description: This work presents a novel method to prepare graphene quantum dots (GQDs) directly from graphite. A composite film of GQDs and ZnO was first prepared using the composite target of graphite and ZnO via magnetron sputtering, followed with hydrochloric acid treatment and dialysis. Morphology and optical properties of the GQDs were investigated using a number of techniques. The as-prepared GQDs are 4–12nm in size and 1–2nm in thickness. They also exhibited typical excitation-dependent properties as expected in carbon-based quantum dots. To demonstrate the potential applications of GQDs in electronic devices, pure ZnO and GQD–ZnO thin-film transistors (TFTs) using ZrO x dielectric were fabricated and examined. The ZnO TFT incorporating the GQDs exhibited enhanced performance: an on/off current ratio of 1.7×107, a field-effect mobility of 17.7cm2/Vs, a subthreshold swing voltage of 90mV/decade. This paper provides an efficient, reproducible and eco-friendly approach for the preparation of monodisperse GQDs directly from graphite. Our results suggest that GQDs fabricated using magnetron sputtering method may envision promising applications in electronic devices.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of one-step fabrication of GQDs/ZnO composite film via magnetron sputtering technique and subsequent preparation of GQDs through acid treatment and dialysis. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) C1s XPS spectra of pristine graphite and the as-prepared GQDs. (b) TEM and HRTEM images, (c) size distribution and (d) HRTEM edge view image of GQDs. Inset in (b) shows the SAED pattern. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) X-ray (002) peak position of GQDs. (b) Raman spectra of as-prepared GQDs. (c) AFM image of the GQDs on Si (1,1,1) substrate. (d) The height profile along the line in (c). (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) UV–vis absorption and (b) photoluminescence spectra of GQDs in water, respectively. Inset in (a) is a photo of GQD aqueous suspension under UV irradiation (365nm). (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

The schematic model and the output curve of (a) pure ZnO TFTs and (b) GQD–ZnO TFTs. (c) Transfer characteristics of corresponding TFTs. (d) XRD patterns of pure ZnO and GQD–ZnO thin films. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Comparison of electrical parameters for ZnO and GQD–ZnO TFTs using ZrO x dielectrics.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots directly generated from graphite via magnetron sputtering and the application in thin-film transistors

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: College of Chemical Science and Engineering, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China (J. Liu).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work presents a novel method to prepare graphene quantum dots (GQDs) directly from graphite. A composite film of GQDs and ZnO was first prepared using the composite target of graphite and ZnO via magnetron sputtering, followed with hydrochloric acid treatment and dialysis. Morphology and optical properties of the GQDs were investigated using a number of techniques. The as-prepared GQDs are 4–12nm in size and 1–2nm in thickness. They also exhibited typical excitation-dependent properties as expected in carbon-based quantum dots. To demonstrate the potential applications of GQDs in electronic devices, pure ZnO and GQD–ZnO thin-film transistors (TFTs) using ZrO x dielectric were fabricated and examined. The ZnO TFT incorporating the GQDs exhibited enhanced performance: an on/off current ratio of 1.7×107, a field-effect mobility of 17.7cm2/Vs, a subthreshold swing voltage of 90mV/decade. This paper provides an efficient, reproducible and eco-friendly approach for the preparation of monodisperse GQDs directly from graphite. Our results suggest that GQDs fabricated using magnetron sputtering method may envision promising applications in electronic devices.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene sheets have been extensively utilized in varied areas owning to its remarkable physical, chemical and mechanical properties [1–5]. However, their easy aggregation, poor dispersion in common solvents, and lack of energy band gap limit some of their direct applications. Recently, there has been an ongoing enthusiasm to provide a deep insight into “small” graphene, also called graphene quantum dot (GQD), which has great potential applications in next-generation electronic and optoelectronic devices due to the quantum confinement and edge effects [6–10]. Compared with large sized graphene or graphite, GQDs are usually biocompatible, strongly luminescent and well dispersed in various solvents [11]. In addition, theoretical and experimental works have shown that quantum confinement and edge effects may introduce band gaps in GQDs, making them an attractive semiconductor material [10,12–15]. The GQDs have been successfully prepared via chemical reduction of GO [16], hydrothermal cutting [17], and electrochemical scissoring of preoxidized graphene sheets [18], or chemical oxidation of carbon black [19]. GQDs can also be synthesized from small molecule precursors like 3-iodo-4-bromoaniline and other substituted benzene derivatives by solution chemistry in a bottom up approach [15]. However, these chemical methods usually cause varying degrees of damage to the structure of GQDs. Magnetron sputtering, as a physical deposition method, is regarded as the best method to deposit oxide films in large-area devices for transparent thin-film transistors (TFTs), photoelectric detectors, and solar cells [20–22]. The unique character of magnetron sputtering technique is the capability to deposit high-packing-density films by the atom-by-atom or molecule-by-molecule transport growth mechanism and nearly flawless structure formation mechanism, at low substrate temperature, which makes it have several advantages such as excellent stoichiometry transfer of the target material, good uniformity, and simple setup required for GQDs preparation [23,24].

ZnO, an n-type semiconductor with wide band gap of 3.37eV, is an important material in the development of electronic devices like light-emitting diodes (LEDs), photovoltaic cells and especially TFTs because of its salient characteristics such as low cost, environment friendliness and good optoelectronic properties [25–27]. Over the past 10years, oxide TFTs have been extensively studied due to the potential use in large area flat-panel display (FPD) applications [28,29]. However, the ZnO films, as the channel layers of TFT, are usually polycrystalline even if formed at room temperature [30]. The grain boundaries deteriorate stability and uniformity of the TFT devices. To address aforementioned issues, great efforts have been made on the composition of the channel layers. Up to now, gallium [31], indium [32], tin [33], zirconium, and lithium [34] have been doped into ZnO thin films to improve the electrical performance of the TFT devices. However, these doped elements are usually rare metals and toxic, therefore, their applications are seriously restricted. GQDs, which have unique electron transportation properties [35], may play an important role in ZnO-based TFT devices. Although GQDs have shown great advantages in various applications like bioimaging [36], optoelectronic devices [18,37,38], pollutant detection [39], and catalysis [40,41], it is a pity that there are hardly any reports on the application of GQDs in TFT.

Herein, we, for the first time, reported the preparation of GQDs directly from graphite via magnetron sputtering and simple chemical treatment, and explored the applications of GQDs in TFTs. The morphology and optical properties of the GQDs were investigated in detail using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), selected area electron diffraction pattern (SAED), Raman spectrometer, atomic force microscope (AFM), UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. To demonstrate the potential applications of GQDs in TFT, pure ZnO and GQD–ZnO TFTs using ZrO x thin film as dielectric were fabricated, and then evaluated carefully.

A composite target of graphite and ZnO was firstly prepared. Graphite powder (0.4g) and ZnO powder (54.0g) were mixed uniformly, before being pressed into a composite target under the pressure of 30MPa using a tablet press. The composite target was subsequently sintered at 1100°C for 4h in nitrogen atmosphere. Silicon substrate plates were cleaned using the standard process of washing in an ultrasonic bath with acetone and methanol, followed by deionized water [42], then dried by nitrogen stream. During sputtering, all parameters were used as follows: target-to-substrate distance was 100mm; base pressure was 1×10−4 mTorr; working pressure was 0.5mTorr; sputtering power was 40W; deposition time was 30min. When deposition ended, the silicon plates were incubated in 1M hydrochloric acid solution for 15min with ultrasonic treatment (power of 100W and nominal frequency of 40kHz at ambient temperature) to dissolve the ZnO part. GQDs were then collected after dialyzing with a cellulose ester membrane bag [MD77(3500)] for 12h. The purified GQDs were re-dispersed in deionized water for further characterizations.

The bottom-gate GQD–ZnO TFTs were fabricated on a heavily doped p-type Si substrate (resistivity∼0.0015Ωcm) using an ultra-thin UV-curved ZrO x (5.5nm) as the dielectric. The preparation method for ZrO x dielectric layer has been proposed in our previous report [29]. The GQD–ZnO channel layer was deposited on ZrO x dielectric using the same sputtering parameters for the preparation of GQDs. 100-nm thick Al thin film was deposited by thermal evaporation to form source and drain electrodes by a shadow mask. The devices have a channel width (W) of 1000μm and a channel length (L) of 250μm. TFTs with pure ZnO channel layer were fabricated in the same way.

XPS spectra were collected on an Escalab 250 X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy with an MgK X-ray source. The TEM observations were performed on a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope with an ultrathin carbon film coated TEM grid operating at 200kV. AFM images were taken using a Veeco D3100 atomic force microscope. The UV–Vis transmission spectra were recorded in the wavelength range 300–800nm using UV–vis−NIR spectrophotometer (Hewlett–Packard, model: 8453). Fluorescence spectroscopy was performed with a Shimadzu RF-5301 PC spectrophotometer. The crystal structures of GQD–ZnO and ZnO thin films on SiO2 substrates, and GQDs (0.2mg/ml) spin-coated on Si substrates were investigated by X-ray diffractometer (XRD, X’Pert-PRO MPD and MRD, PANalytical, Holland) with a CuKα1 radiation. Raman spectra were recorded on a Raman spectrometer (Jobin–Yvon HR 800 France). The carrier concentrations in pure ZnO and GQD–ZnO films were characterized by Hall-effect measurement using a van der Pauw configuration. The electrical properties of as-fabricated TFTs based on ZrO x dielectric were measured by using a semiconductor parameter analyzer (Agilent 4155C) in a dark box.

The GQDs were prepared via magnetron sputtering combined with simple acid treatment and dialysis. As shown in Fig. 1 , the composite target of graphite and ZnO in a vacuum chamber was bombarded by the argon ions produced by gas ionization. The sputtered graphene and ZnO were then concomitantly deposited on silicon substrate to afford a homogeneous composite film. After magnetron sputtering the substrate was incubated in hydrochloric acid solution with ultrasonic treatment, through which deposited ZnO was selectively dissolved. After dialysis, the dissolved ZnO (ZnCl2) was removed, leaving GQDs uniformly dispersed in water solution. The reasons for why a composite target of graphite and ZnO was used instead of pure graphite are dual: the first one is to avoid the aggregation of GQDs during the deposition process, and the second one is the low oxidative property of dilute hydrochloric acid solution, which only caused insignificant oxidation of GQDs as evidenced by the XPS spectra for both graphite precursor and GQDs (Fig. 2 a). It can be noticed that the peak of GQDs was slightly broader and more asymmetric compared to that of pure graphite, which might be resulted from the trace hydroxyl and carboxyl groups generated during the hydrochloric acid treatment [17].

The microstructure of the GQDs was analyzed by TEM and HRTEM. Fig. 2b and c shows the TEM image of GQDs and their size distributions. The size distribution was figured out by measuring the size of two hundred of particles as observed in TEM images. The average size of the GQDs was calculated to be 8.0±1.5nm. The HRTEM image in Fig. 2b indicated the high crystallinity of the GQDs and a lattice parameter of 0.21nm, which was consistent with the (102) diffraction planes of sp2 graphitic carbon [43]. The edge view image of the obtained GQDs is shown in Fig. 2d. It can be seen that these GQDs mostly consist of 3–5 layers of graphene. It is worth noting that the adjacent graphene layers are almost parallel, similar to those of graphite, indicating that nearly no significant oxidation of GQDs occurred during the fabrication process [44]. The crystallinity of these GQDs was further checked by selected area electron diffraction pattern. The hexagonal pattern (Fig. 2b inset) showed the sixfold symmetry of carbon atoms arranged in graphene plane, indicating good crystallinity of the few layered GQDs [45]. Both the TEM and the SAED pattern evidenced that these GQDs are highly crystalline carbon nano-materials with graphitic structure.

Fig. 3 a gives the XRD characterization result of GQDs. A broad intense peak (002) centered at 2θ ≈26.0° (d =0.34nm, with the full-width at half-maximum (FWHM) of 0.893) can be observed obviously. The size of GQDs was calculated to be 9.028nm using Scherrer equation, a size which is similar to that observed with TEM. These GQDs were smaller than those synthesized by hydrothermal cutting of GSs [17], nanolithography technique [35], chemical exfoliation [46], and precursor pyrolysis [46]. The quality of the as-prepared GQDs was also confirmed by Raman spectroscopy (Fig. 3b). The D band at around 1358cm−1 and the G band at around 1597cm−1 were observed obviously. Unlike the much more intense D band as observed with the hydrothermally synthesized GQDs [17], the relative intensity of the “disorder” D-band and the crystalline G-band (I D/I G) for the as-prepared GQDs is only around 0.7, which not only indicates the high quality of these GQDs, but also the uniqueness of the magnetron sputtering method developed here for GQD preparation. The AFM images of the GQDs are shown in Fig. 3c. The topographic height distribution obtained from the AFM analysis revealed that most GQDs were approximately 1.5nm in thickness (Fig. 3d), suggesting that these GQDs contains 3–5 layers of graphene, a result which is consistent with the HRTEM result and comparable to the previous reports [16,17].

The UV absorption of the GQDs was found to be at ca. 340nm (Fig. 4 a), similar to the reported values [46,47]. In addition, bright blueviolet fluorescence was visually observed when the GQD suspension was irradiated with 365nm UV light (inset in Fig. 4a), which is one of the unique optical properties of GQDs compared to large-scale graphene, mainly due to the quantum confinement and edge effects of GQDs [6–10]. The GQDs also exhibited excitation-dependent photoluminescent (PL) behaviors. As shown in Fig. 4b, when the GQD suspension was excited at wavelengths from 320 to 480nm, the PL peak consistently shifted from 360nm to 570nm but with remarkably decreasing PL intensity. The PL spectra with whole wavelengths are shown in Fig. S1. Upon the excitation at the absorption band of 340nm, the PL spectrum showed a strongest peak at 386nm. This excitation-dependent PL behavior may be resulted from the optical selection of differently sized GQDs and surface states of GQDs [48–50], a phenomenon which was also commonly observed with other fluorescent carbon-based QDs [17,18,36,51–53]. The XPS analyses for GOD-ZnO films and finally obtained GQDs were carried out and no Zn or Cl elements were observed with the as-prepared GQDs, indicating that these optical properties were only resulted from the GQDs, rather than ZnO impurities. The XPS spectra are shown in Fig. S2.

In order to clarify the effect of GQD on the electrical properties of ZnO thin films, the pure ZnO TFT and GQD–ZnO TFT were fabricated. Fig. 5 a and b shows the output curves of the ZnO and GQD–ZnO TFTs at various gate voltages, from 0 to 2.5V in a step of 0.5V. Both TFT devices exhibited a typical n-channel behavior with clear pinch-off voltage and current saturation without showing current crowding phenomena, indicating good Ohmic contact between metal electrodes and the channel layers. It was noted that the GQD–ZnO TFT exhibited a saturation current of 45μA at 2.5V, which was nearly 9 times larger than that of pure ZnO TFT. Fig. 5c shows the corresponding transfer curves of the as-fabricated TFTs and the electrical parameters of pure ZnO and GQD–ZnO TFTs are summarized in Table 1 . The threshold voltage (V TH) was defined by fitting a straight line to the plot of the square root of I DS versus V GS. The field-effect mobility (μ FE) can be calculated in the saturation region (V DS > V GS − V TH) using the following Eq. (1):
(1)
I
DS
=
μ
FE
C
i
W
2 L
(
V
GS
-
V
TH
)
2
where C i, L, and W are the areal capacitance of the ZrO x dielectric and the channel length and width of the TFT, respectively. The SS is defined as the V GS required increasing the I DS by a factor of 10 using the following Eq. (2):
(2)
SS =
dV
GS
d ( log
I
DS
)

Compared with pure ZnO TFT, the GQD–ZnO TFT exhibited better electrical properties, including a higher μ FE of 17.7cm2/Vs, a larger on/off current ratio (I on/I off) of 1.7×107, and a smaller SS value of 90mV/decade. These properties meet the requirements for the applications in e-book and TFT-LCD. Particularly, all these parameters were obtained at a low operating voltage of 2.5V, which is significant for low-power electronic devices. Electrical properties of TFT devices fabricated with GQDs–ZnO films having different GQD contents have been provided in the Supporting Information (Table S1), while keeping the same GQDs–ZnO film thickness of 50nm.

Since the dielectric in both TFTs was ZrO x , the enhanced electrical properties of GQD–ZnO TFT may derive from microstructure variation of the GQDs doped ZnO thin films. To confirm that, the XRD measurements were carried out first. The pure ZnO thin films exhibited a crystalline phase with (002) and (103) peaks at 34.13° and 61.81°, respectively [54,55], whereas the GQDs doped ZnO only showed (002) crystalline peak with dramatically decreased intensity, and the (103) crystalline peak was completely disappeared (Fig. 5d). As a consequence, when the charge carrier transports in GQD–ZnO channel layer, the less grain boundary scatter leads to a larger on-state current (large I on/I off) and fast-speed opening (small SS value). It has been reported that the introduction of carbon (doping) into the ZnO lattice could lead to the increase in carrier concentration [56,57]. As expected, the carrier concentrations were observed to increase significantly, from n =6.7×1016/cm3 for the pure ZnO film, to n =7.5×1017/cm3 for the GQD–ZnO film, which is consistent with the larger saturation current for GQDs–ZnO TFTs.In addition, the good structural integrity as evidenced by Raman spectrum (Fig. 3b) endowed the GQDs with highly conjugated and crystallized structure and unique excellent electrical conductivity, making these GQDs much better electron transfer relays to enhance the performance of TFTs.

The magnetron sputtering and acid dissolution method developed here provides a facile, reproducible and eco-friendly approach for the preparation of monodisperse GQDs directly from graphite. In light of the good dispersion, size uniformity and easy processing, GQDs fabricated via magnetron sputtering method can be potentially put into mass production and also extensively used in varied applications of lasing, bioimaging, photovoltaic devices, and LEDs. Compared with TFT fabricated with pure ZnO, the GQD–ZnO TFTs exhibited significantly enhanced on/off current ratio, field-effect mobility, and sub-threshold swing voltage. It is envisioned that our endeavor may significantly broaden the applications of GQDs in varied areas.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: JL acknowledge the Natural Science Foundation of China (51173087), Natural Science Foundation of Shandong (ZR2011EMM001) and Taishan Scholars Program for financial support. WR acknowledge Deakin CRGS funds.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2015.03.007.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: This document file contains Supplementary Figs. S1, S2, and Table S1.
